preamble: The transformation from a voiceless aspirated retroflex affricate (ʈʂʰ) to a voiceless postalveolar fricative (ʃ) is a well-documented phonetic shift, primarily occurring as a lenition process. This change involves the simplification of the sound by losing its affricate quality, dropping the aspiration, and shifting its place of articulation from retroflex to postalveolar. This type of sound change is observed in various languages and dialects, particularly within the Sinitic branch of the Sino-Tibetan family.
phoneticEffects: This shift encompasses de-affrication (affricate to fricative), loss of aspiration, and a change in the place of articulation from retroflex to postalveolar, which can be described as a form of de-retroflexion or slight fronting

examples.note: In Taiwan Guoyu, the retroflex aspirated affricate [ʈʂʰ] from Putonghua (Standard Mandarin) may be realized as the postalveolar fricative [ʃ]. This phenomenon is also found in other Mandarin dialects across southern and parts of northern China.
